metadata.summary: Identify and remove idle cloud resources (unattached volumes, orphaned snapshots, stopped instances) and schedule non-production environments to stop during off-hours for 65-70% non-prod savings.

content.title: Cloud Waste Elimination and Non-Production Scheduling
content.summary: Cloud waste elimination targets idle and orphaned resources that generate cost with zero value: unattached EBS volumes, orphaned snapshots, stopped instances, unused Elastic IPs, and idle load balancers. Non-production scheduling shuts down dev, staging, and QA environments during off-hours, achieving 65-70% savings on non-prod compute. These are the fastest FinOps quick wins — implementation in hours, savings immediate.

why.p: Idle resources accumulate invisibly. A developer launches a test instance, forgets it, and it runs for 6 months. A snapshot retention policy is never set, and snapshots from deleted volumes persist for years. Non-production environments run 24/7 despite being used only during business hours — paying for 168 hours per week when 50 are needed.

A real enterprise case: staging ($40,000/month), development ($35,000/month), and QA ($15,000/month) all running 24/7. After implementing scheduling, the combined non-prod bill dropped to $28,200/month — a 69% reduction with no engineering effort beyond initial setup.

list.item: First FinOps action in any environment — waste elimination has zero risk and immediate ROI.
Non-production environments that are only used during business hours or weekdays.
Any environment where a resource audit has not been performed in the past 30 days.
Before a quarterly FinOps review — clean waste first, then analyze the remaining spend meaningfully.
Automated deletion without a dry-run step first — always audit before destroying.
Non-production environments that require 24/7 availability for on-call, customer demos, or automated overnight jobs — define explicit exceptions before enabling scheduling.

section.name: idle-resource-categories
section.p: Idle resource categories and typical quick-win commands:

example.title: Find unattached EBS volumes (AWS CLI)
example.code:
# Find unattached volumes (status=available means not attached to any instance) aws ec2 describe-volumes \ --filters Name=status,Values=available \ --query 'Volumes[*].{ID:VolumeId,Size:Size,Type:VolumeType,CreateTime:CreateTime}' \ --output table
# Typical savings: $500-5,000/month depending on environment size # Action: snapshot then delete, or delete directly if clearly orphaned

example.title: Find unused Elastic IPs (AWS CLI)
example.code:
# Find unassociated Elastic IPs (charged $3.60/IP/month when not attached) aws ec2 describe-addresses \ --query 'Addresses[?AssociationId==null].{IP:PublicIp,AllocationId:AllocationId}' \ --output table
# Release unused EIPs aws ec2 release-address --allocation-id eipalloc-xxxxxxxx
example.note: Small individual savings ($3.60/IP/month) but easy wins. Large environments accumulate dozens of orphaned EIPs.

example.title: Find stopped instances (AWS CLI)
example.code:
# Find instances stopped for more than 7 days aws ec2 describe-instances \ --filters Name=instance-state-name,Values=stopped \ --query 'Reservations[].Instances[*].{ID:InstanceId,Type:InstanceType,StopTime:StateTransitionReason,Tags:Tags}' \ --output table
# Note: stopped instances still incur EBS storage charges # Terminate if not needed; storage charges stop only after termination

rule.statement: All non-production environments (dev, staging, QA, sandbox) MUST implement scheduled shutdown during off-hours. Minimum schedule: stop at 20:00, start at 06:00 on weekdays; stop Friday 20:00, start Monday 06:00 for weekends. Target 70% cost reduction on non-prod compute.
rule.rationale: Non-production environments are used during business hours only. Running them 24/7 wastes 65-70% of their cost. The AWS Instance Scheduler, Lambda cron, or Terraform scheduled actions implement this in under 2 hours with 65-70% immediate savings.

example.title: AWS Instance Scheduler configuration
example.code:
# Development schedule: weekdays 08:00-20:00 dev-schedule: periods: - name: weekday-work-hours begintime: "08:00" endtime: "20:00" weekdays: "mon-fri" timezone: "Europe/Warsaw"
# Staging schedule: broader window for automated tests staging-schedule: periods: - name: business-hours-plus begintime: "06:00" endtime: "22:00" weekdays: "mon-fri"
# Results (real case): # Staging: $40,000 → $13,500/month (66% savings) # Development: $35,000 → $10,200/month (71% savings) # QA: $15,000 → $4,500/month (70% savings) # Total non-prod: $90,000 → $28,200/month (69% savings)

example.title: Non-production environments running 24/7
example.code: # Staging: $40,000/month running 24/7 # Used: weekdays 09:00-18:00 = 45 hours/week out of 168 hours total # Usage rate: 27% # Waste rate: 73% # Annual waste: ~$352,000 in staging alone
example.note: A single non-production environment running 24/7 when used 9 hours/day is wasting 73% of its compute cost. Scheduling is the fastest FinOps ROI available.

section.name: cleanup-automation
section.p: Cleanup automation prevents waste from re-accumulating after the initial sweep:

rule.statement: Implement automated idle resource detection to run weekly. Alerts MUST include: resource ID, age, estimated monthly cost, and last-active date. Cleanup MUST require human approval for production resources; non-production cleanup can be automated with a 7-day warning period.
rule.rationale: Manual audits decay — after the initial cleanup sprint, the process is forgotten and waste re-accumulates within 3-6 months. Automated weekly scanning with human-readable alerts keeps the pipeline clean continuously.

example.title: Lambda cleanup script skeleton (Python)
example.code:
import boto3 from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta
def find_idle_resources(dry_run=True): ec2 = boto3.client('ec2') findings = []
# Unattached EBS volumes volumes = ec2.describe_volumes( Filters=[{'Name': 'status', 'Values': ['available']}] )['Volumes'] for vol in volumes: age_days = (datetime.now(timezone.utc) - vol['CreateTime']).days if age_days > 7: monthly_cost = estimate_ebs_cost(vol['Size'], vol['VolumeType']) findings.append({ 'type': 'unattached_volume', 'id': vol['VolumeId'], 'age_days': age_days, 'monthly_cost': monthly_cost, 'action': 'delete' if dry_run is False else 'review' })
return findings
# Always run with dry_run=True first findings = find_idle_resources(dry_run=True) for f in findings: print(f"{f['type']}: {f['id']} | {f['age_days']}d old | ${f['monthly_cost']:.0f}/mo")
example.note: Always implement dry-run mode. Running cleanup without preview destroys legitimate resources and erodes trust in FinOps automation.

section.name: network-waste
section.p: Network cost waste is often overlooked but significant at scale:

list.item: Keep compute and data in the same region — cross-region data transfer is billed both directions
Use VPC Endpoints to avoid NAT Gateway charges for S3 and DynamoDB access
Implement CDN for static content — CloudFront egress is cheaper than EC2 egress
Review NAT Gateway usage — NAT Gateway charges per GB of data processed, not just hourly
Audit API Gateway usage — idle API Gateways with no traffic still incur monthly charges

section.name: success-metrics
section.p: Success metrics for waste elimination program:

list.item: Cloud waste reduction: target above 25% of baseline spend
Spot adoption for eligible workloads: target above 50%
Non-prod scheduled savings: target 70% reduction on non-prod compute

section.name: prompts
section.prompt:
Analyze this resource inventory for potential waste:
[Paste resource list with utilization metrics]
Categories to check: - Unattached volumes - Unused load balancers - Idle instances (< 5% CPU for 7+ days) - Orphaned snapshots - Unused Elastic IPs - Oversized databases
For each category, provide: 1. Count of wasteful resources 2. Monthly cost of waste 3. Confidence level (high/medium/low) 4. Recommended action (terminate/review/monitor) 5. Automation script or CLI command to clean up

Design a scheduling strategy for non-production environments:
Environments: [List environments with current 24/7 costs]
Usage patterns: - Development: [describe when used] - Staging: [describe when used] - QA: [describe when used]
Constraints: - Timezone: [timezone] - On-call requirements: [any] - Exception process needed: [yes/no]
Please provide: 1. Recommended schedule per environment 2. Expected savings calculation 3. Implementation approach (Instance Scheduler, Lambda, etc.) 4. Exception handling for after-hours access 5. Monitoring and alerting setup 6. Sample schedule configuration

Generate a cleanup script for [AWS/GCP/Azure] to remove:
Resources to clean: - Unattached EBS volumes older than [X] days - Snapshots older than [X] days without tags - Stopped instances for more than [X] days - Unused Elastic IPs
Requirements: - Dry-run mode first - Logging of all actions - Exclude resources with specific tags: [list]
Output: 1. Bash/Python script 2. IAM permissions required 3. Scheduling recommendation (cron/Lambda) 4. Rollback procedure

checklist.check: Audit for unattached EBS volumes and delete or snapshot-then-delete
Find and release orphaned Elastic IPs
Identify stopped instances older than 7 days and terminate or restart
Review orphaned snapshots older than 90 days without associated volumes
Audit idle load balancers with zero traffic
Check for unused RDS instances (stopped but still accruing storage cost)
Implement scheduled shutdown for all non-prod environments
Target 70% non-prod cost reduction via scheduling
Set up automated weekly idle resource detection with human-readable alerts
Implement S3 lifecycle policies for log buckets and temp storage
Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering for buckets with variable access patterns
Review NAT Gateway usage and implement VPC Endpoints where applicable
Set up automated dev environment auto-stop after N hours of inactivity
